angle.headline: Why Your M&A Diligence Process Finds Everything Except What Kills Deals
angle.thesis: Middle-market owners spend six figures on diligence that audits the past but never diagnoses whether the target can operate without its founder — the single variable that determines post-close performance.
angle.targetPersona: Business owners in growth-through-acquisition mode, particularly those who've completed one deal and are now considering their second, operating with $10M-$75M revenue across the US-LATAM corridor
angle.relevanceToAudience: With USMCA at its six-year review and nearshoring momentum creating acquisition opportunities across the corridor, owners are evaluating targets in Mexico and LATAM — but standard diligence frameworks were built for US-only deals and miss the founder-dependency risk that is magnified across borders and jurisdictions.

peopleAngle.challenge: The seller's leadership team has never operated without the founder's real-time input, and the buyer's integration team lacks the trust or context to make decisions during the 90-day transition window when founder dependency becomes operationally visible.
peopleAngle.framework: Kotter Step 2: Build the Guiding Coalition

justification.angleChoice: Most M&A diligence answers 'Does this business make money?' but never asks 'Can this business make decisions without the seller in the room?' — the real post-close question.
justification.osPillarChoice: The failure happens because diligence is structured to audit the past (financials, compliance, IP) rather than diagnose the operating model's ability to execute independently — particularly across time zones and legal systems in corridor deals.
justification.peopleAngleChoice: The Machine Test: before you sign the LOI, spend one week asking only this: 'Who makes what decisions when the owner is unavailable?' If the answer is 'it waits' or 'it escalates,' you are buying a job, not a business.

entries.fieldReportSummary: [Meta announced this week](https://www.businessinsider.com/meta-layoffs-internal-memo-ai-job-cuts-faq-2026-5) that it will cut 10% of staff on Wednesday, eliminating around 8,000 roles in three waves of notifications timed to 4am local time across regions. angle.headline: The 30-Second Filter: Why Your Best Hires Never Get Past the Interview
angle.thesis: Business owners who personally interview every candidate believe they're protecting quality, but they've actually made themselves the bottleneck — the same instinct that built the business now prevents it from scaling.
angle.targetPersona: Founder-led businesses with $10M-$50M revenue where the owner still personally approves every key hire and complains the talent pool has deteriorated
angle.relevanceToAudience: SpaceX's radical interview protocol exposes the paradox of founder-led hiring: the same judgment that identified early winners becomes the constraint preventing the next 100 hires — especially critical for corridor operators building cross-border teams where cultural fluency matters more than résumé pedigree
talkingPoints: You can't complain about talent quality when you're the reason qualified candidates walk away — a 30-second interview that ends badly isn't screening for excellence, it's screening for desperation

Musk's method works at SpaceX because he built the hiring machine first: structured rubrics, trained interviewers, clear go/no-go criteria that don't require him in every room — most owners skip that step and wonder why nothing scales
The Delegation Threshold framework: you can personally interview up to 50 employees; beyond that, you're either building an interviewing system or building a 50-person company

Pick one role this quarter where you will not interview the final candidate — design the hiring rubric, train the interviewer, review the decision after the fact, and learn whether your business can make a good call without you

peopleAngle.challenge: The owner must trust that someone else can identify talent as well as they can — which requires admitting that their personal judgment, while valuable, is not the only valid filter and may actually encode biases that limit the team's diversity and capability

entries.fieldReportSummary: LongRange Capital is acquiring Pizza Hut from Yum Brands for $1.5 billion, [according to Middle Market Growth's PE Weekly](https://middlemarketgrowth.org/pe-weekly-pizza-hut-take-private-tech-deals/), with Yum China Holdings buying the Mainland China business for $1.2 billion.
